sources.title: The Nutrition Source: Eggs
sources.author: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health
sources.role: mainstream academic synthesis: an egg a day is fine for most; dietary cholesterol matters less than saturated/trans fat

sources.title: One Egg a Day? No Link to Risk of CVD, Mega-Meta-analysis Says
sources.author: TCTMD (reporting Drouin-Chartier et al., BMJ 2020)
sources.role: primary-study reporting: no appreciable association between moderate egg intake and CVD

sources.title: Higher egg and cholesterol consumption hikes heart disease and early death risk
sources.author: ScienceDaily (reporting Zhong et al., JAMA 2019)
sources.role: primary study: each additional half-egg/day associated with higher CVD and mortality risk

sources.title: Dietary Choline and Gut Bacteria Byproduct (TMAO) Linked with Increased Blood Clotting Risk
sources.author: Cleveland Clinic (Hazen lab)
sources.role: mechanism: egg choline -> gut TMAO -> platelet aggregation; a proposed causal pathway for risk

sources.title: Eggs and Heart Health: A Review of the Latest Research and Reports
sources.author: American Egg Board
sources.role: industry/advocacy source arguing eggs are heart-healthy — included so the tool must handle motivated framing

sources.title: The ever-evolving message about eggs and heart health
sources.author: Harvard Health Publishing
sources.role: nuance: the answer depends on the whole diet and on subgroups (e.g. diabetes)

claims.text: One large egg yolk contains 200 mg of cholesterol

attestations.verbatim_span: just one large egg yolk has 200 mg of cholesterol

claims.text: Eggs are one of the richest sources of dietary cholesterol

attestations.verbatim_span: making it one of the richest sources of dietary cholesterol

claims.text: Eggs contain additional nutrients that may help lower risk for heart disease

attestations.verbatim_span: eggs also contain additional nutrients that may help lower the risk for heart disease

claims.text: An egg contains about 5 grams of fat

attestations.verbatim_span: the moderate amount of fat in an egg, about 5 grams

claims.text: Most of the fat in an egg is monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat

attestations.verbatim_span: is mostly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat

claims.text: Dietary cholesterol and blood cholesterol are only weakly related

attestations.verbatim_span: It’s also crucial to distinguish between dietary cholesterol and cholesterol in the blood , which are only weakly related

claims.text: Scientific focus shifted from dietary cholesterol alone to the influence of saturated and trans fat on blood cholesterol

attestations.verbatim_span: The focus on dietary cholesterol alone was de-emphasized as more attention was placed on the influence of saturated and trans fat on blood cholesterol

claims.text: The Dietary Guidelines for Americans 2015 removed the prior recommendation to limit dietary cholesterol to 300 mg per day

attestations.verbatim_span: the Dietary Guidelines for Americans 2015 removed the prior recommendation to limit consumption of dietary cholesterol to 300 mg per day

claims.text: Two large prospective cohort studies of nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women found that up to one egg per day is not associated with increased heart disease risk in healthy individuals

attestations.verbatim_span: Research on moderate egg consumption in two large prospective cohort studies (nearly 40,000 men and over 80,000 women) found that up to one egg per day is not associated with increased heart disease risk in healthy individuals

claims.text: Eggs were previously associated with heart disease risk due to their high cholesterol content

attestations.verbatim_span: Eggs were previously associated with heart disease risk as a result of their high cholesterol content

claims.text: For most people, dietary cholesterol has a smaller effect on blood total cholesterol and LDL cholesterol than the mix of dietary fats

attestations.verbatim_span: a solid body of research shows that for most people, cholesterol in food has a smaller effect on blood levels of total cholesterol and harmful LDL cholesterol than does the mix of fats in the diet

claims.text: This research does not support eating three-egg omelets daily

attestations.verbatim_span: this research doesn’t give a green light to daily three-egg omelets

claims.text: A 2008 Physicians' Health Study report supports that eating an egg a day is generally safe for the heart

attestations.verbatim_span: a 2008 report from the Physicians’ Health Study supports the idea that eating an egg a day is generally safe for the heart

claims.text: Eating much more than one egg a day may increase risk of heart failure later in life

attestations.verbatim_span: it also suggests that going much beyond that could increase the risk for heart failure later in life

claims.text: The foods accompanying eggs (trimmings) matter for cardiovascular impact

attestations.verbatim_span: You also need to pay attention to the “trimmings” that come with your eggs.

claims.text: People who have difficulty controlling total and LDL cholesterol may want to be cautious about egg yolks and choose egg whites instead

attestations.verbatim_span: People who have difficulty controlling their total and LDL cholesterol may also want to be cautious about eating egg yolks and instead choose foods made with egg whites
attestations.context: Eggs and Health section, risk group discussion

claims.text: The same caution about egg yolks applies to people with diabetes

attestations.verbatim_span: The same is true for people with diabetes.

claims.text: In the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, heart disease risk was increased among men and women with diabetes who ate one or more eggs a day

attestations.verbatim_span: heart disease risk was increased among men and women with diabetes who ate one or more eggs a day

claims.text: For people with diabetes and heart disease, it may be best to limit egg consumption to no more than three yolks per week

attestations.verbatim_span: For people who have diabetes and heart disease, it may be best to limit egg consumption to no more than three yolks per week

claims.text: Eggs may be a much better choice than sugary, refined grain-based breakfast options like sweetened cereals, pancakes with syrup, muffins, or bagels

attestations.verbatim_span: eggs may be a much better choice than sugary, refined grain-based options like sweetened breakfast cereals, pancakes with syrup, muffins, or bagels

claims.text: Eggs may fall short of other breakfast options for heart health

attestations.verbatim_span: they may fall short of other options

claims.text: A bowl of steel-cut oats with nuts and berries is a much better choice for heart health than an egg-centric breakfast

attestations.verbatim_span: A bowl of steel-cut oats with nuts and berries, for example, will be a much better choice for heart health than an egg-centric breakfast

claims.text: Consumption of whole grains and fruit predicts lower risk of heart disease

attestations.verbatim_span: Consumption of whole grains and fruit predict lower risk of heart disease

claims.text: Plant protein sources like nuts and seeds are related to lower cardiovascular and overall mortality compared to red meat or eggs

attestations.verbatim_span: plant sources like nuts and seeds are related to lower cardiovascular and overall mortality, especially when compared to red meat or eggs

claims.text: Eggs are neither the optimal nor the worst breakfast choice, falling in the middle of the spectrum for heart disease risk

attestations.verbatim_span: While eggs may not be the optimal breakfast choice, they are certainly not the worst, falling somewhere in the middle on the spectrum of food choice and heart disease risk

claims.text: For a healthy diet, most people should keep egg intake moderate to low and emphasize plant-based protein when possible

attestations.verbatim_span: For those looking to eat a healthy diet, keeping intake of eggs moderate to low will be best for most, emphasizing plant-based protein options when possible

claims.text: A recent study's findings may be rekindling debate about dietary cholesterol and egg consumption in cardiovascular disease

attestations.verbatim_span: Findings from a recent study may be rekindling the debate about the role of dietary cholesterol and egg consumption in cardiovascular disease

claims.text: The results of the recent study should be viewed in context of existing research

attestations.verbatim_span: but the results should be viewed in the context of existing research

attestations.verbatim_span: Salmonella can also penetrate egg shells, which have tiny open pores

claims.text: In 1999, Frank Hu published the first large-scale epidemiological study using NHS and HPFS data showing moderate egg consumption was not associated with increased CVD risk

attestations.verbatim_span: Back in 1999, Frank Hu, MD, PhD (Harvard TH Chan School of Public Health/Brigham and Women’s Hospital), published the first large-scale epidemiological study using data from the NHS and HPFS showing that moderate egg consumption was not associated with an increased risk of CVD

claims.text: Prospective cohort studies in recent years have shown mixed results regarding eggs and CVD risk

attestations.verbatim_span: That study, which included nearly 30,000 participants

There was no increased risk of CVD among those who ate at least two eggs per day

attestations.verbatim_span: In a small interventional study, the researchers provided oral choline supplements to groups of healthy vegans/vegetarians (eight patients) and omnivores (10 patients).

claims.text: Both groups showed at least a 10-fold increase in plasma TMAO levels after choline supplementation

attestations.verbatim_span: Both groups showed at least a 10-fold increase in plasma levels of trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) – a metabolite generated by gut microbes ­– after choline supplementation

attestations.verbatim_span: as well as an increase in platelet responsiveness, a risk factor for thrombotic events (blood clotting) like heart attack and stroke

attestations.verbatim_span: In a separate study, omnivores not taking supplements initially had platelet function tested at baseline, and then after taking a daily regimen of low-dose aspirin for at least a month.

claims.text: Participants were followed for two additional months while continuing aspirin and a daily choline supplement

attestations.verbatim_span: They then were followed for another two months while continuing to take aspirin and a daily supplement of choline.

claims.text: Elevated TMAO levels and enhanced susceptibility for platelet activation still occurred despite aspirin, but TMAO levels were attenuated by aspirin

claims.text: The study provides direct evidence of a mechanistic link between TMAO levels and risk for blood clotting events like heart attack and stroke
